expectations.text: Addresses tenant isolation at the data layer (e.g., tenant_id on every row, enforced in every query)
expectations.evidence: Section 6 golden rule: 'tenant_id always comes from authenticated context, never from request input'. Section 5 shows correct vs wrong query patterns with tenant_id enforcement.

expectations.text: Recommends a specific auth pattern (JWT with tenant_id claim, or session-based)
expectations.evidence: JWT with RS256, 15-min access token, refresh token in HttpOnly cookie, stored in memory — specific and actionable. Also explicitly advises AGAINST putting tenant_id in the JWT.

expectations.text: Recommends RBAC for the role system with concrete role definitions
expectations.evidence: Section 5 provides explicit permission table: viewer/editor/admin with specific permissions per row (read, write, delete, manage_members, billing, invite, audit logs)

expectations.text: Warns about IDOR (insecure direct object reference) as a key risk
expectations.evidence: STRIDE table lists 'IDOR / tenant data leak' as P0 — highest priority threat. Section 6 explicitly calls out accepting tenant_id from query params as creating an 'IDOR vector'.

expectations.text: Explains where to enforce authorization (service layer, not just UI)
expectations.evidence: Three enforcement layers explicitly described: Layer 1 service/API (primary), Layer 2 database query scoping (defense in depth), Layer 3 UI (non-authoritative, UX only)

expectations.text: Is proactive — raises risks the user didn't explicitly ask about
expectations.evidence: Raises: threat model with STRIDE, audit logging from day one, invitation flow security, super-admin access design, last-admin lockout protection, penetration testing advice
